Burrow architects.<[silence:800ms]> Grassland sculptors.<[silence:800ms]>

The word rabbit comes from Old French raboit, meaning "to dig"—fitting for animals that reshape soil like tiny, furred bulldozers.<[silence:800ms]>

A rabbit’s life is a race against hunger and predators.<[silence:800ms]> Their teeth grow 10 centimeters a year, forcing them to gnaw constantly, turning meadows into patchwork quilts of clipped grass.<[silence:800ms]> Unlike deer, which browse selectively, rabbits shear plants clean, pruning ecosystems with surgical precision.<[silence:800ms]> Their hind legs act like coiled springs:<[silence:100ms]> a fleeing cottontail can zigzag at 72 kilometers per hour, outmaneuvering foxes by changing direction mid-air.<[silence:800ms]>

Warren systems—labyrinths of tunnels with multiple exits—function as climate-controlled bunkers.<[silence:800ms]> In winter, shared body heat keeps burrows 10 Celsius degrees warmer than the surface.<[silence:800ms]> Kits (babies) are born blind and furless but grow faster than most mammals;<[silence:100ms]> a newborn jackrabbit can outrun a human in three weeks.<[silence:800ms]>

Domestication twisted their fate.<[silence:800ms]> Medieval monks bred them for fur and meat, accidentally creating the first pet rabbits.<[silence:800ms]> Today, the European rabbit’s invasive descendants in Australia have reshaped continents, their numbers checked only by engineered viruses.<[silence:800ms]>

Three related subjects are digging as survival, herbivore teeth evolution, and invasive species warfare.<[silence:800ms]>
clean_story:
Burrow architects. Grassland sculptors.

The word rabbit comes from Old French raboit, meaning "to dig"—fitting for animals that reshape soil like tiny, furred bulldozers.

A rabbit’s life is a race against hunger and predators. Their teeth grow 10 centimeters a year, forcing them to gnaw constantly, turning meadows into patchwork quilts of clipped grass. Unlike deer, which browse selectively, rabbits shear plants clean, pruning ecosystems with surgical precision. Their hind legs act like coiled springs: a fleeing cottontail can zigzag at 72 kilometers per hour, outmaneuvering foxes by changing direction mid-air.

Warren systems—labyrinths of tunnels with multiple exits—function as climate-controlled bunkers. In winter, shared body heat keeps burrows 10 Celsius degrees warmer than the surface. Kits (babies) are born blind and furless but grow faster than most mammals; a newborn jackrabbit can outrun a human in three weeks.

Domestication twisted their fate. Medieval monks bred them for fur and meat, accidentally creating the first pet rabbits. Today, the European rabbit’s invasive descendants in Australia have reshaped continents, their numbers checked only by engineered viruses.

Three related subjects are digging as survival, herbivore teeth evolution, and invasive species warfare.
